title: Interview Templates
description: These are the five most reusable algorithmic templates in coding interviews. Memorize the skeleton, then adapt to specific problems. Sliding window, binary search, two-pointer, prefix sum, and backtracking cover the majority of interview patterns.

sections.title: Sliding window
sections.explanation: The sliding window maintains a range [left, right] that expands by moving right and shrinks by moving left. Use it for subarray/substring problems with a contiguous constraint: max sum of size k, longest substring without repeats, minimum window containing target chars. O(n) time.

exercise.prompt: Find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of size 3 in [2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2]. Use the fixed sliding window pattern. Print the max sum.

validation.message: Use the sliding window pattern with Math.max to track the maximum

exercise.hint: Slide by adding nums[i] and subtracting nums[i - k]. Max window is [5,1,3] = 9.

sections.title: Binary search
sections.explanation: Binary search halves the search space each step for O(log n) time. The template uses `left = 0, right = arr.length - 1` and computes `mid = Math.floor((left + right) / 2)`. Adjust left or right based on comparison. Works on sorted arrays and monotonic functions.

exercise.prompt: Implement binary search on sorted array [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13]. Search for 7 (print the index) and for 6 (print -1).

validation.message: 7 is at index 3, 6 is not found (-1)
Use the standard binary search template with left, right, and mid

exercise.hint: Continue while left <= right. Narrow with left = mid + 1 or right = mid - 1.

sections.title: Two-pointer technique
sections.explanation: Two pointers move inward from both ends (or at different speeds) to solve problems in O(n) time. Classic uses: pair sum on sorted arrays, container with most water, palindrome check, and removing duplicates. The key insight is that pointer movement eliminates possibilities without checking them all.

exercise.prompt: Use two pointers to check if a string is a palindrome. Test with "racecar" (true) and "hello" (false). Print each result.

validation.message: Use left and right pointers moving inward in a while loop

exercise.hint: Continue while left < right. Increment left++, decrement right--.

sections.title: Prefix sum
sections.explanation: A prefix sum array stores cumulative sums so any subarray sum can be computed in O(1): sum(i, j) = prefix[j+1] - prefix[i]. Build it in O(n), then answer range queries instantly. This pattern appears in subarray sum problems, running averages, and difference array techniques.

exercise.prompt: Build a prefix sum for [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]. Print the prefix array joined by commas. Then compute and print the subarray sum from index 1 to 3 (inclusive).

validation.message: Build the prefix sum array by pushing cumulative sums

exercise.hint: Add each number to the last prefix value. Range sum [1,3] = prefix[4] - prefix[1].

sections.title: Backtracking template
sections.explanation: Backtracking systematically explores all candidates and prunes invalid paths. The template: for each option, choose it, recurse deeper, then unchoose it. Pruning conditions skip invalid branches early. This solves permutations, combinations, subsets, N-Queens, and constraint satisfaction problems.

exercise.prompt: Generate all permutations of [1, 2, 3] using backtracking. Print the count, then print each permutation as a comma-joined string on its own line (sorted lexicographically).

validation.message: Use pop() to unchoose and used array to track selections

exercise.hint: path.pop() undoes the choice, used[i] = false unmarks the element.
